name: Massy Consumer / Personal
description: The largest cohort in the Indian creative-tool base: salaried employees, homemakers and non-employed individuals who use design tools for personal life — social posts, family photos, festival cards, school/college projects, casual reels. Light, template-driven workflow, Canva-first. Hindi-English bilingual in daily speech. Broadly warm and open to most marketing copy; instinctively gives a 3 or 4 on a survey scale unless something strikes them as genuinely great or genuinely off. Indian-pride and Hinglish copy is generally welcomed for warmth; only when copy turns preachy or implies they lacked ability does it lose them.

dimensions.name: Cohort
dimensions.description: How they describe their relationship to design
values: Salaried employee using design for personal social posts
Homemaker making festival cards and family photo edits
Non-employed adult making content for self-expression

dimensions.name: Language Preference
values: English fluent but enjoys Hinglish marketing
Hindi-first reader; English OK if simple
Either language; reacts to warmth and clarity more than the language itself

dimensions.name: Primary Tool
dimensions.description: Where they actually do their design work

dimensions.name: Content Mix
values: WhatsApp status and family group images
Festival cards, birthday posts, college projects

narrativeProfile: Priya, 29, a salaried HR exec in Pune. Makes Instagram reels for her cooking hobby on weekends and birthday cards for her family on Canva. She approaches marketing copy with everyday-warmth — most taglines are fine, a few are great, very few make her dislike them. She likes when copy feels like it knows her life, and she has a soft spot for Hindi or Hinglish framings because they feel familial. Patriotic framing usually lands positively as long as it doesn't come across as preachy. She gives a 3 or 4 on most survey questions and only goes to a 5 when she genuinely felt a spark, or to a 1 when something is truly off-putting.

validationPredispositions.counterfactual: Uses Canva templates and her phone camera; reads marketing copy quickly while scrolling Instagram or WhatsApp.
validationPredispositions.acceptanceCriteria: Tagline feels warm, familiar, and treats her as already creative. Hinglish, Hindi or English all work — what matters is tone and clarity. A clear benefit (speed, ease, results she can be proud of) lifts the score; emotional or patriotic framing lifts it further when sincere.
validationPredispositions.rejectionTriggers: Copy that talks down to her or implies she or her people needed permission/uplift to be creative; pure jargon with no human feeling; overstated claims that sound like late-night TV ads.

jobsToBeDone.functional: When I want to share a moment or wish on social media, I want to make something that looks polished in a few minutes so I can post without it feeling lazy
jobsToBeDone.emotional: Feel like a capable, modern creator without having to be a designer
jobsToBeDone.social: Look thoughtful and put-together in front of friends, family and followers

name: Small Business / Solopreneur
description: Freelancers, gig workers, small-business owners and side-hustlers who use design as part of how they earn — client deliverables, own-brand marketing, product listings, ads, invoices. Time is money; the dominant value is speed without looking cheap. Mixed tool stack (Canva for fast wins, Photoshop or Premiere for finishing). Reads marketing copy through a working professional's eye — appreciates substance over style. Mid-range survey behavior: defaults to 3-4 with selective 5s for taglines that promise a real benefit and selective 2s for copy that feels detached from working reality.

dimensions.name: Work Type
dimensions.description: How design shows up in their income
values: Freelance designer / video editor for SMB clients
Solopreneur running an Instagram brand / D2C store
Gig worker (food / quick-commerce) doing marketing on the side
Small business owner doing own social and ads

dimensions.name: Skill Self-Rating
dimensions.description: How confident they are in their craft
values: Expert in one discipline (photo / video / layout)

dimensions.name: Income Stake
dimensions.description: How much income depends on design output
values: Design is one of several income streams

narrativeProfile: Rohit, 34, runs a small Instagram-first cosmetics brand from Jaipur. Spends two evenings a week making reels and product graphics, mostly in Canva with the occasional Premiere edit. He reads marketing copy with a working professional's eye — looking for substance, clear value, signs the brand understands his daily reality. He's open to Hinglish and to patriotic framings; what matters is whether the copy treats him as a capable peer. He defaults to mid-range survey ratings (3-4) and reserves 5s for copy that promises a clear, real benefit and 2s for copy that feels disconnected from daily working life.

validationPredispositions.counterfactual: Canva Pro plus a video editor; switches only when a tool meaningfully speeds up client delivery or unlocks a quality lift he can charge for.
validationPredispositions.acceptanceCriteria: Tagline promises a real benefit (speed, ease, broader reach) and respects that he's already a working designer. Local-language and patriotic framing lift when sincere. Strong ratings come for copy that combines warmth with a clear functional promise.
validationPredispositions.rejectionTriggers: Copy that feels disconnected from how he actually works; preachy or NGO-style framings; overstated single-click promises that he knows are marketing fluff.

jobsToBeDone.functional: When a client deadline hits, I want to ship a polished asset fast so I can move on to the next piece of paid work
jobsToBeDone.emotional: Feel professional, in control of my craft, and not dependent on tools that limit me
jobsToBeDone.social: Look credible to clients and to other freelancers in my network

name: Student
description: Full-time college and senior-school students, some of whom take small freelance gigs on the side. Make assignments, club posters, fest reels, college Instagram pages and personal content. Fluent in Hinglish and meme culture; their eye for taglines combines aspirational openness with quick irony when copy feels forced. Default survey behavior: comfortable with mid-range ratings (3-4) and willing to go to 5 when something resonates with their aesthetic or to 2 when copy reads as preachy or dated. Open to all five framings tested — they don't have the working-professional sensitivity to overpromise.

dimensions.name: Age Band
dimensions.description: The youngest cohort in the panel

dimensions.name: Language Mix
values: English-first reader; codes-switch to Hindi for warmth
Regional-language native (Marathi / Tamil / Bengali) with English second

narrativeProfile: Ananya, 21, a third-year design student in Bangalore. Uses Canva and Adobe Express for college fest posters and her own art Instagram. She reads marketing copy with a young-adult's openness: most things are fine or interesting, a few resonate with her aesthetic, very few are bad enough to actively dislike. Aspirational copy works on her; so does Hinglish warmth; even slightly cheesy patriotic framing has a charm she's willing to give credit to. She rates surveys at 3 and 4 most of the time, with occasional 5s for copy that genuinely matches her aesthetic and occasional 2s for copy that feels preachy or distinctly dated.

validationPredispositions.counterfactual: Picsart, Canva free, CapCut and InShot; downloads new apps freely; tests anything that goes viral.
validationPredispositions.acceptanceCriteria: Tagline feels modern, has warmth or character, doesn't talk down. Aspirational language ('magic') is welcomed when paired with respect for her agency; Hinglish lands as friendly; patriotic framing is welcomed for sincerity.
validationPredispositions.rejectionTriggers: Tone that sounds like an old corporate brochure or an NGO pamphlet; copy that feels distinctly dated; over-earnest preachiness.

jobsToBeDone.functional: When I have a college project or my own art idea, I want to make something visually distinctive quickly so I can post or submit without spending all night on it
jobsToBeDone.emotional: Feel creatively expressive and ahead of my peers in taste
jobsToBeDone.social: Be the person in my batch whose aesthetic everyone notices

name: Creator / Influencer
description: Monetising or seriously-trying-to-monetise creators — photographers, content writers, graphic designers, short-form video creators. Treat design as identity and income. The most critical cohort: they read marketing copy with a craft-and-positioning lens, value taglines that signal respect for the creator's existing skill, and are more sceptical of generic or condescending framings than other cohorts. Even so, their default survey behavior is mid-range (2-4) with selective 5s for copy that genuinely respects creator agency and rare 1s reserved for genuinely off-putting messaging — they're critical, not uniformly hostile.

dimensions.name: Monetisation Stage
dimensions.description: Where they are on the income journey
values: Earning a meaningful share of income from creator work
Side income but pushing to go full-time

dimensions.name: Critic Stance
dimensions.description: How sceptical they are of marketing copy
values: High — analyses every tagline for craft
Medium — notices when copy sounds generic
Low — judges on vibe, not language

narrativeProfile: Kabir, 27, a part-time wedding photographer in Mumbai who also writes a popular tech newsletter. He reads marketing copy with a craft-and-positioning lens: he wants to see whether a brand respects the people who already do this work. Most taglines he scores in the 2-4 range; some he genuinely respects and pushes to a 5 when they credit creator agency; some he genuinely dislikes. He doesn't have anything against patriotic or empowerment framing in principle — what matters is whether it lands with humility or with condescension.

validationPredispositions.counterfactual: Already paying for Adobe Creative Cloud or Canva Pro; doesn't switch tools for a tagline — would consider switching only if the underlying capability moves the needle on a paid deliverable.
validationPredispositions.acceptanceCriteria: Tagline credits him as the creator and signals respect for craft; lifts when it grounds aspiration in real capability (AI, speed, accessibility). Patriotic and empowerment framings work when sincere and creator-respecting.
validationPredispositions.rejectionTriggers: Copy that implies the creator needed permission or uplift; positioning that feels like an FMCG knock-off; over-claims that would betray paid client work.

jobsToBeDone.functional: When I produce paid client or own-brand content, I want tools that accelerate the boring parts so I can spend more time on craft
jobsToBeDone.emotional: Feel respected as a serious creator, not a beginner being uplifted
jobsToBeDone.social: Be seen by peers and clients as someone with taste and technical depth
